summary: Cortez's affordable-housing pipeline is anchored by the 42-unit Tawi Kaan permanent supportive housing project (CHFA 2022 Round One 9% LIHTC, 2025 Edson Award winner), a sequence of 2023-2025 policy actions (Housing Needs Assessment, Housing Action Plan, Proposition 123 opt-in, full Land Use Code rewrite, Park Village RFQ), and the regional Housing Authority of Montezuma County (MCHA, ~221 units across Cortez, Dolores, and Mancos).

paragraphs.text: Tawi Kaan Apartments (560 E. Empire Street) is the modern flagship Cortez LIHTC project. CHFA reserved Tawi Kaan a 9% federal LIHTC allocation in 2022 Round One (announced May 18, 2022) of approximately $1,233,450 in annual federal credits, supporting a 42-unit, 3-story permanent supportive housing development for households at or below 30% AMI. Per the CHFA Rifle Apartments 2023 narrative, Tawi Kaan was the first competitive LIHTC built in Cortez in many years.

Developers were BlueLine Development (Boulder) in partnership with The Piñon Project Family Resource Center (Cortez); architect Shopworks Architects; tax-credit equity investor Boston Financial. Grand opening was October 15, 2024.

Tawi Kaan received the 2025 Charles L. Edson Tax Credit Excellence Award in the Native American Housing category from the Affordable Housing Tax Credit Coalition; the award was presented on Capitol Hill on October 27, 2025.
The City adopted a Housing Needs Assessment in May 2023 and a Housing Action Plan later in 2023. The HNA documented that from 2020 to 2022, Cortez housing costs increased by 15.7% per year.

Cortez established its Proposition 123 baseline in a City Council presentation on October 10, 2023 and filed its 3-year affordability commitment by the November 1, 2023 state deadline. The DOLA-published 3-year stock-growth targets in the Cortez-area baseline-sharing group are: Cortez +89 units; Mancos +14; Dolores +26; the surrounding county +28; and the tribal jurisdiction +5.

The City received a $133,120 Local Planning Capacity (LPC) Grant under Prop 123 / DOLA-DLG to fund a dedicated Housing Coordinator position to implement the HAP, inventory sites, and broker developer relationships.

The City Council unanimously adopted a comprehensive new Land Use Code on March 11, 2025 (effective April 18, 2025), replacing the 1996 code. Key housing provisions: a 90-day decision clock for qualifying affordable housing applications (Prop 123 fast-track), a 20% density bonus in the R2 / R3 zones when more than 10% of units are deed-restricted affordable / workforce, and a rezone of the former Manufactured Home District to Mixed Residential in south Cortez.

sections.heading: Park Village Housing RFQ — West 7th Street

paragraphs.text: On March 26, 2025 the City issued a Request for Qualifications for the Park Village Housing development — approximately 7 acres of a 14-acre city-owned former Montezuma-Cortez High School site on the north side of West 7th Street between South Oak Street and South Chestnut Street; the western 7 acres of the parcel are designated as parkland. Bids were due May 6, 2025. The City's stated target is housing serving households between 80% and 120% AMI.

The Housing Authority of Montezuma County (MCHA) is the principal regional public housing entity for Cortez. MCHA operates 10 properties and 221 units across Cortez, Dolores, and Mancos, with service area extending to La Plata County. The portfolio spans Public Housing, Section 8 PBRA, and tax-credit set-asides.

Cortez-located MCHA properties include Brubaker Place Apartments (691 Wyoming Street, 48 units — 8 1-BR, 24 2-BR, 16 3-BR — FHA-insured plus a LIHTC 40/60 set-aside), Prairie Mesa Estates (650 E 2nd Street, 30 units — 12 2-BR, 18 3-BR), Sleeping Ute Apartments (316 / 516 S Madison Street, 60 units — 35 2-BR, 20 3-BR, 5 4-BR — Section 8 PBRA plus LIHTC 20/50), and Cortez Apartments (620 E Empire Street, Section 8 PBRA plus affordable units).

USDA Rural Development maintains a Colorado field office in Cortez at 628 W 5th Street (ZIP 81321), one of six Colorado RD field offices, covering the southwestern region. Per the USDA-RD eligibility map, 100% of the surrounding county is RD-eligible for both Single Family (502) and Multifamily (515 / 514 / 516 / 538) programs.

Region 9 Economic Development District (which absorbed the Southwest Colorado Council of Governments in 2022) serves the five-county southwestern region and the two area tribes. Region 9 received a recent CHFA / DLG Housing Planning Grant to build a standardized automated Housing Needs Assessment template to help rural jurisdictions comply with SB24-174.

HomesFund — a regional homebuyer-assistance nonprofit — serves the Cortez homeownership pipeline. HomesFund received approximately $3.545M in Prop 123 Affordable Homeownership equity (2024 state award) shared across the five southwestern counties.
